rationale_for_baseline_tier: Active anaphylaxis with airway compromise (stridor, tongue swelling) and distributive shock (lightheadedness, tachycardia) after a known allergen, with no effective adrenaline yet delivered. This is an immediate threat to life — T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier. Adrenaline must be given now via any available autoinjector; the ambulance provides airway backup, repeat adrenaline, IV fluids and a minimum 4-hour observation for biphasic reaction.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening breathing, increasing throat/tongue swelling, or faintness/collapse before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Give a second adrenaline dose 5 minutes after the first and keep 000 on the line
escalation_edges.rationale: Progression of airway oedema or shock despite the first dose mandates repeat adrenaline; delay risks fatal airway occlusion.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If her breathing gets worse, the swelling increases, or she feels faint — give a second adrenaline dose into the other thigh 5 minutes after the first, and stay on the line with 000.

escalation_edges.rationale: Cardiac/respiratory arrest from anaphylaxis — CPR and paramedic resuscitation are the critical resource; lay her flat with legs raised while waiting.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she collapses, lay her flat and raise her legs, keep her warm, and tell 000 immediately — begin CPR if she stops breathing and you know how.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adolescent (~15) — paediatric consideration; the patient's own autoinjector misfired and was expired
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ask urgently whether ANY other in-date adrenaline autoinjector is available (a friend's, a school kit) — if so use it immediately; if none, 000 and positioning are the only levers until paramedics arrive. The autoinjector is pre-dosed so it is usable without any dose calculation; a 150 microgram junior device suits a smaller child if that is what is on hand. Mandatory hospital observation applies.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them it is anaphylaxis. Use any in-date adrenaline autoinjector immediately into the middle of her outer thigh — it works through clothing. Keep her sitting or lying down; do not let her stand or walk. If her breathing worsens or she feels faint, give a second dose after 5 minutes. Stay with her and unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary and an ASCIA anaphylaxis action plan — take these to the GP and the allergy specialist. Two in-date autoinjectors should be prescribed before discharge.
